title: Roast Leg of Pork
subtitle: A traditional Sunday joint with sage and onion stuffing and proper crackling
excerpt: Leg of pork is larger and leaner than shoulder, making it the classic slicing joint for a formal Sunday table. Bone-in or boned and rolled, it roasts in 2-3 hours and delivers clean, slightly firmer slices alongside the obligatory crackling.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: roasting leg of pork with sage stuffing. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845): sage and onion forcemeat. Traditional British Sunday lunch practice.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Score and salt the skin 24 hours ahead, leaving uncovered in the fridge. The stuffing can be made and baked in a separate dish during the last 40 minutes of the roast.
recipe.temperatureNote: initial blast, then 180°C for the remainder

glossaryTerms.definition: A mixture of finely chopped or minced ingredients, usually including herbs, onion, and fat, used to stuff or accompany a roast. Sage and onion is the classic British forcemeat for pork.

content.text: A bone-in leg of pork is a substantial joint that suits a larger gathering. Unlike shoulder, which benefits from very long, slow cooking, the leg has less fat marbling and cooks more quickly at a moderate temperature. The result is firmer, cleaner slices with a crust of salt-blistered crackling on top.
The traditional accompaniment is sage and onion
, baked in a separate dish and served in spoonfuls alongside. Some households stuff it into the cavity if the butcher has boned the leg; others bake it separately for a crispier top.

items.prepNote: leg joint, bone-in or boned and rolled, skin on, scored

content.text: firmly into the score lines and over the skin; it should feel like a salt crust. Drizzle
over the skin and rub in the
. Leave the joint uncovered in the fridge for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight.
Make the sage and onion stuffing
in a frying pan over medium heat. Add the
and cook gently for 10 minutes until soft. Off the heat, stir in
. Season well. Bind with the beaten egg. Tip into a small buttered baking dish.

Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Roast the pork for 30 minutes to blister the crackling. Drop the temperature to 180°C (160°C fan) and continue for roughly 25 minutes per 500 g. The leg is done when the thickest point reads 75°C on a probe. Add the stuffing dish to the oven for the last 40 minutes.
Transfer the joint to a warm board. Rest for 20 minutes. Pour off excess fat, leaving the fond. Add
to the pan, scrape well, and reduce for 5 minutes. Strain into a warm jug.

rows.symptom: Crackling is tough rather than crisp
rows.cause: Skin was not dry enough, or the initial blast was not hot enough
rows.fix: Ensure the skin is dry before seasoning. If the crackling is still tough after cooking, score it away from the joint and grill it on a foil-lined tray for 5 minutes.

rows.cause: Leg is a lean cut that dries out past 80°C
rows.fix: Use a probe and pull the joint at 75°C. Serve with plenty of gravy and stuffing to compensate for slight dryness.

rows.symptom: Stuffing is soggy in the middle
rows.cause: Too much egg, or not spread thinly enough in the dish
rows.fix: Spread no more than 2-3 cm deep. Remove foil for the last 10 minutes to crisp the top.

rows.symptom: Pan juices are too fatty for a clean gravy
rows.cause: Pork leg releases more fat than expected
rows.fix: Pour juices into a tall jug, allow fat to rise to the surface (2-3 minutes), and spoon it off before making gravy.

content.text: Roast leg of pork with sage and onion stuffing is the pork equivalent of the Sunday rib: formal enough for a gathering, straightforward enough for any confident cook. The crackling is the prize; the stuffing is the complement. Serve with roast potatoes, apple sauce, and a green vegetable.
